summary: The middle ear (tympanic cavity) communicates with the nasopharynx via the auditory (Eustachian) tube.
The middle ear communicates directly with the external auditory meatus via a permanent opening.
The middle ear is a completely sealed cavity with no communication to other spaces.
The facial nerve (CN VII) passes through the middle ear in the medial wall, making it vulnerable in chronic otitis media.
The facial nerve has no relation to the middle ear and is never at risk during ear surgery.
The trigeminal nerve (CN V) is the nerve most commonly damaged in middle ear infections.
Otitis media in children is common because the auditory tube is shorter, wider, and more horizontal than in adults.
Otitis media is common because children have a longer and narrower auditory tube.
The auditory tube has no role in the spread of infection to the middle ear.
The inner ear contains the bony labyrinth (filled with perilymph) and the membranous labyrinth (filled with endolymph).
The inner ear contains only one fluid called cerebrospinal fluid.
Endolymph fills the bony labyrinth while perilymph fills the membranous labyrinth.
Arnold's nerve (auricular branch of CN X) innervates part of the ear canal and can trigger a cough reflex during ear examination.
Arnold's nerve is a branch of CN V3 that supplies the tragus only.
The cough reflex during ear examination is caused by irritation of the glossopharyngeal nerve.
